title: Kundalini and Laughter That Won't Stop: The Manic Joy of Awakening
meta_description: You're probably reading this because you're scared, aren't you? It's 3 AM, your heart's doing something weird, and you just laughed so hard you thought you
body:
Kundalini and Laughter That Won't Stop: The Manic Joy of Awakening

You're probably reading this because you're scared, aren't you? It's 3 AM, your heart's doing something weird, and you just laughed so hard you thought you might burst, or maybe you've been laughing for hours and you can't stop. It doesn't feel right. It feels manic, out of control. You're alone, and you're wondering if you're losing your damn mind. I get it. I've been there. That uncontrollable, almost terrifying joy? Yeah, I know that one. It's not just a laugh; it's a force, a tremor that shakes your whole being. It's not the kind of laughter you share with friends over a beer. This is something else entirely. It's raw, it's primal, and it can feel utterly insane. You're not alone, believe me. What you're experiencing could be a symptom of a kundalini awakening, and that's a whole different ballgame than just being happy. It's intense. It's disorienting. And it can make you feel like you're on the edge of a cliff, teetering between enlightenment and insanity. But here's the thing: you're still here, you're looking for answers, and that's a good sign. It means a part of you is fighting, trying to make sense of the chaos. Hold onto that part. It's your anchor.

I remember my first real bout of it. I was sitting in my apartment, trying to meditate, trying to find some peace, and suddenly, this wave of pure, unadulterated joy just hit me. It wasn't gentle. It was like a tidal wave. I started laughing, and I couldn't stop. My stomach hurt, my face ached, and tears were streaming down my face, but it wasn't sadness. It was this overwhelming, ridiculous hilarity. It went on for what felt like forever. I tried to shut it off, to control it, but it was like trying to stop a freight train with my bare hands. It was happening to me, not by me. And in the back of my mind, this tiny, terrified voice was screaming, "You're going crazy!" Right? You know that voice. It's loud, it's persistent, and it sounds an awful lot like your own. But it's not the truth. It's fear. It's the ego's last stand against something it can't comprehend, something that threatens its carefully constructed reality. This kind of laughter, this manic joy, it's often a release. A pressure valve blowing off. All that pent-up energy, all that old trauma, all those suppressed emotions - it's finding a way out. And sometimes, it comes out as laughter that feels like it's going to crack you open. And in a way, it is. It's cracking you open to something new, something bigger. And that's terrifying, but it's also incredibly powerful. You're not just laughing; you're shedding, you're transforming. It feels like a breakdown, but it's often a breakthrough. Know what I mean? It's a rough ride, but you're not going to break. You're stronger than you think. You've made it this far, haven't you?

What the Hell is Happening? Understanding Kundalini Phenomena

So, what exactly is kundalini? You've probably heard the term, maybe read a few scary stories online. Look, it's not some fluffy New Age concept. It's a powerful, ancient force described in many spiritual traditions. Imagine a coiled serpent at the base of your spine - that's the classic imagery. When it "awakens," it's said to rise, bringing with it intense energy and deep changes to your consciousness and your body. It's not always a gentle unfolding. For many, it's a shockwave. And that shockwave can manifest in all sorts of wild ways. The uncontrollable laughter is just one of them. You might also experience intense heat or cold, involuntary movements (kriyas), buzzing or vibrating sensations, visions, sounds, emotional rollercoasters, and yes, moments of pure, blissful, almost terrifying joy. It's like your entire operating system is being upgraded, but you didn't get the manual, and the upgrade process feels like a system crash. Does that land? It's disorienting because your body and mind are trying to process an energy that's completely outside your normal experience. It's like trying to run a supercomputer program on a potato. There's a mismatch, and that mismatch creates friction, discomfort, and sometimes, those manic episodes. I've seen it described in books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) - Sannella documents a bunch of these wild experiences. You're not alone in these sensations, not by a long shot.

The problem is, our Western medical system isn't really equipped to deal with this. You go to a doctor, describe uncontrollable laughter or full-body tremors, and they're likely to diagnose you with some mental illness, prescribe you meds, and send you on your way. And sometimes, those meds can make things worse, or at least suppress the energy without resolving the underlying process. It's a catch-22. You need support, but the typical support systems don't understand what you're going through. That's why finding people who get it, who've been through it, is so important. You're not sick in the conventional sense, though it sure as hell feels like it. You're undergoing a deep spiritual transformation. And those transformations aren't always pretty or comfortable. They're messy, they're disruptive, and they can make you question everything you thought you knew about yourself and reality. So yeah, that feeling of being completely out of control? It's a feature, not a bug, of this process. It's your system recalibrating, clearing out the old, making space for the new. And sometimes, that clearing out is hilarious in a completely unhinged way. Or terrifying. Or both at the same time. It's a spectrum, and you're probably somewhere on it right now.

The Overwhelming Nature of Bliss

Let's talk about the bliss for a second. Because it's not just regular happiness. This is a different beast entirely. When kundalini energy flows, it can bring with it incredible states of joy, love, and connection. It can feel like you're plugged directly into the source of all existence, like every cell in your body is vibrating with pure light. It's ecstatic. It's overwhelming. And when it gets too intense, when your system can't quite hold that much pure, unadulterated joy, it can spill out as manic laughter. It's like your internal circuits are overloaded with positive voltage, and the laughter is the safety switch. It's a beautiful thing, in its own wild way, but it's also a lot for the human nervous system to handle. Imagine suddenly feeling the infinite love of the universe coursing through you. Your small, egoic mind isn't built for that. It tries to process it, to categorize it, to control it, and it can't. So it short-circuits, and you laugh. You laugh because it's too much, it's too good, it's too absurd, it's too everything. It's a release of that immense pressure. And sometimes, that pressure just keeps coming, and so does the laughter. You might feel like you're going to explode with joy, like your heart will literally burst. I've felt that. It's a strange, beautiful terror. You're not just happy; you're touching something divine, and it's rattling your cage. This is what Bonnie Greenwell talks about in When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening (paid link) - the intensity and unexpectedness of these experiences. She also has The Kundalini Guide (paid link) which is another good read if you want more perspective.

The Shadow Side: When Joy Becomes Mania

Now, here's the honest truth: sometimes, that manic joy can tip into something less pleasant. It can feel like actual mania, like you're losing touch with reality. You might feel unstoppable, invincible, like you don't need sleep, like you're receiving cosmic messages. And while some of that can be part of the awakening, it can also become genuinely destabilizing. This is where discernment comes in. Is it a temporary, intense release of joy, or is it starting to feel like a sustained state where your judgment is impaired? Are you making impulsive decisions? Are you alienating loved ones? Are you neglecting basic self-care? If the answer to any of those is yes, then you need to be careful. The line between spiritual ecstasy and psychological distress can get blurry during these times. That's why grounding practices are so important (we'll get to those). You're not trying to stop the kundalini, but you are trying to integrate it safely, to give your system time to adjust. You're trying to build a bigger container for all that energy, so it doesn't just overflow and short-circuit everything. It's a delicate dance, balancing the wildness of the awakening with the need for stability in your everyday life. So yeah, be honest with yourself about where you are on that spectrum. It's okay to admit if it's getting too much. That's not a sign of weakness; it's a sign of wisdom.

Why Is This Happening to Me?

You're probably asking yourself that a lot, aren't you? Why me? Why now? And there's no single, easy answer. For some, it's years of intense spiritual practice - meditation, yoga, breathwork. For others, it's a sudden, spontaneous event triggered by stress, trauma, a near-death experience, or even just a deep desire for truth. It doesn't always make sense. It's not always something you actively sought. Sometimes, it just happens. It's like your system decides it's time for an upgrade, whether you're ready or not. And once it starts, you can't really put the genie back in the bottle. You have to learn to work with it, to integrate it. Think of it like this: you've been living your life with a certain amount of energy flowing through you. Suddenly, someone cranks the dial up to 11. Your circuits aren't designed for that, not yet. So you feel overwhelmed, you feel manic, you feel out of control. It's a natural reaction to an unnatural influx of energy. Your body and mind are trying to adapt, trying to expand to accommodate this new reality. And that process is by nature messy and uncomfortable. It's a stripping away of the old, a burning through of anything that's not truly you. And sometimes, that burning feels like laughter that won't quit. It's a release, a purgation. It's not punishment; it's purification. And it's happening to you because, on some level, you're ready for it, even if your conscious mind is screaming bloody murder. You're ready to shed the old skin and step into something new. It's a big deal, and it's tough, but you're tougher. Remember that.

Surviving the Manic Joy: What You Can Do

Okay, so you're laughing your head off at 3 AM, your body's buzzing, and you feel like you're losing your mind. What now? You can't just stop it, right? But you can learn to manage it, to ground it, to integrate it so it doesn't completely derail your life. Here's what I learned, often the hard way:

  • Grounding Practices: This is your lifeline. You need to connect back to the earth, to your physical body.
    • Walk in nature: Barefoot if you can. Feel the earth under your feet.
    • Eat nourishing food: Root vegetables, heavy, grounding foods. Stay away from stimulants like caffeine and sugar.
    • Cold showers: Sounds awful, I know, but it can snap you back into your body.
    • Physical labor: Chop wood, garden, clean your house. Anything that gets you out of your head and into your hands.
    • Deep breathing: Focus on slow, deep breaths into your belly. This helps regulate your nervous system.
  • Movement: Don't just sit there trying to suppress it. Let the energy move.
    • Gentle yoga or stretching: Focus on opening your hips and spine.
    • Dancing: Just let your body move freely, no judgment.
    • Shaking: Literally shake your body to release tension and energy.
  • Express Yourself (Safely): That energy needs an outlet.
    • Journaling: Write down everything you're experiencing, no filter. Get it out of your head and onto paper.
    • Creative expression: Paint, draw, sing, play an instrument. Whatever helps you channel that intense energy.
    • Talk to someone who gets it: This is HUGE. Find a therapist or a spiritual counselor who understands kundalini or spiritual emergence. Look for resources like those mentioned in The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) for approaches to spiritual crisis.
  • Rest, but Don't Avoid: You might feel like you don't need sleep, but you do. Your nervous system is working overtime. Try to create a calming bedtime routine. But don't use sleep or isolation as a way to avoid what's happening. You have to face it, even if it's terrifying.
  • Mind Your Thoughts: Your mind will try to make sense of everything, and it will often catastrophize. When those "you're going crazy" thoughts come, acknowledge them, but don't feed them. Remind yourself that this is a temporary, intense phase of a spiritual process.
  • Hydration: Drink plenty of water. This energy can be dehydrating.
  • Community: If possible, find a community of people who are also going through spiritual awakening. Knowing you're not alone makes a world of difference. Online forums, local groups - whatever you can find.

You're not trying to stop the laughter or the energy. You're trying to contain it, to channel it, to integrate it so it doesn't overwhelm you. It's like learning to ride a wild horse. You don't break the horse; you learn to ride it. You learn to move with its power, to guide it, rather than letting it drag you wherever it wants to go. It's a journey of learning to be present with intense sensations, to witness them without judgment, and to trust that there's a deeper intelligence at play, even when it feels completely chaotic. It's hard work, the hardest work you'll ever do, honestly. But it's also the most rewarding. Because on the other side of this chaos, there's a deeper peace, a more deep understanding of who you are, and a connection to something much bigger than yourself. You're not just surviving; you're evolving. And that's something to hold onto when the laughter gets too loud and the fear creeps in. You've got this. One breath, one moment, one laugh at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
